title: Softer Rates and Bigger Risks Are Reshaping the US P&C Market
gist: US P&C insurers are transitioning from a hard market to a soft-rate environment while structural risks—social inflation, SCS-driven catastrophe losses, and unpriced AI liability—compound. The gap between top- and bottom-quartile performers is widening as AI adoption, underwriting precision, and scale economics increasingly determine which carriers capture durable TSR. BCG's analysis covers 2021–2025 actual performance and projects the next three-plus years.

themes.theme: Market Cycle Transition: Hard to Soft

insights.claim: US P&C insurers delivered five-year average annual TSR of approximately 18% for 2021–2025, outperforming the global insurance average of 15% and most other sectors, but 2025 one-year TSR decelerated relative to European and Asia-Pacific peers as pricing softens.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: US P&C outperformance era is fading; rotation toward European or APAC insurers may offer better risk/reward as the US cycle turns down.
insights.quote: US P&C insurers delivered five-year average annual TSR of approximately 18% for the period from 2021 through 2025, outperforming the global insurance average of 15%
insights.locator: TSR Performance: Strong but Moderating

insights.claim: Premium rates are softening across personal auto and commercial property in the US P&C market, forcing carriers to compete on data, segmentation, and expense discipline rather than simply riding rate increases.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Carriers without AI-driven underwriting or superior data assets will see margin compression; differentiate holdings toward data-infrastructure leaders.
insights.quote: Premium rates are softening across multiple lines, particularly personal auto and commercial property.
insights.locator: After several years of strong performance

insights.claim: Tangible book value growth driven by underwriting income was the primary engine of US P&C value creation from 2021 to 2025, with multiple expansion muted as markets price in a softer cycle.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: TBV growth will slow as underwriting margins compress; P/TBV multiples already discount cycle turn, limiting further multiple expansion.
insights.quote: The primary engine of value creation over the 2021–2025 period remains growth in tangible book value (TBV), the result of strong underwriting income.
insights.locator: TSR Performance: Strong but Moderating

insights.claim: The carrier transition from a hard to a soft market is historically the most strategically consequential period in the insurance cycle; carriers that maintain underwriting discipline during this shift consistently outperform over the subsequent five-year period.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Screen for carriers that are not chasing volume at the expense of margin; disciplined underwriters today will dominate the next hard market.
insights.quote: Carriers that maintain underwriting discipline during this shift—resisting the temptation to grow premium volume at the expense of margin—consistently outperform over the subsequent five-year period.
insights.locator: Master the Softening Cycle Transition

insights.claim: Top-quartile US P&C companies generated an estimated underwriting RoTE contribution of approximately 11 percentage points from 2021 to 2025, while bottom-quartile companies reported negative underwriting contributions.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: The 11+ ppt spread between top and bottom quartile is a persistent alpha source; concentrate in top-quartile underwriters, avoid mid-tier carriers with opaque reserve quality.
insights.quote: top-quartile P&C companies generated an estimated underwriting RoTE contribution of approximately 11 percentage points, while the bottom-quartile reported negative underwriting contributions.
insights.locator: Underwriting Determines the Profitability Divide

insights.claim: Elevated net investment income in 2024–2025 partially masked underwriting deterioration at weaker US P&C carriers; as interest rates normalize, underwriting quality will re-emerge as the primary profitability differentiator.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Rate normalization is a negative catalyst for weak-underwriting carriers that relied on investment income as a buffer; watch reserve adequacy disclosures closely.
insights.quote: Elevated net investment income in 2024–2025 partially masked the underwriting deterioration at weaker carriers. As interest rates normalize and investment income falls, underwriting quality will remain the primary distinguishing factor for profitability.
insights.locator: Underwriting Determines the Profitability Divide

themes.theme: Personal Auto: Cycle Recovery and Competitive Re-Escalation

insights.claim: US personal auto combined ratios improved from 112% in 2022 to 92% in 2025—the best since 2020—representing the first combined ratio below 95% since 2004 outside of the COVID-19 reduced-driving period.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Personal auto profitability is fully restored, signaling the end of re-underwriting tailwinds; forward returns in this line depend on holding margin as competitors re-enter.
insights.quote: Combined ratios in personal auto improved to 92% in 2025 from 112% in 2022, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence and NAIC data.
insights.locator: From Defense to Offense in Auto

insights.claim: Progressive reduced its personal auto combined ratio to approximately 88% in 2025, became the number one US personal auto insurer, and captured significant premium growth while competitors remained in repair mode after moving first to reprice.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Progressive's first-mover repricing advantage is a template: early disciplined action in a distressed line creates disproportionate market share gains in the recovery; watch for analogous moves in casualty.
insights.quote: Progressive, which moved first and most decisively to reprice its book, recovered profitability substantially ahead of the rest of the market. The company reduced its personal auto combined ratio to approximately 88% in 2025.
insights.locator: From Defense to Offense in Auto

insights.claim: Annual loss costs in US personal auto are estimated at 6% to 8% by industry actuaries, outpacing rate increases in some states and compressing margins even as the market appears recovered.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: The profitability recovery in personal auto is thinner than headline combined ratios suggest; loss cost trajectory is a leading indicator of the next round of rate hardening.
insights.quote: Annual loss costs (estimated at 6% to 8% by industry actuaries) are outpacing rate increases in some states, compressing margins.
insights.locator: From Defense to Offense in Auto

insights.claim: Large mutual insurers, unconstrained by quarterly investor expectations, may absorb margin pressure to gain personal auto market share in the current soft cycle, presenting a structural competitive threat to publicly listed carriers.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Public P&C carriers cannot match mutuals on patience; combined ratio discipline will erode faster than consensus expects in competitive re-entry phases.
insights.quote: Competitive intensity from large mutuals remains significant. Unconstrained by quarterly investor expectations, mutual insurers may be willing to absorb margin pressure to increase market share.
insights.locator: From Defense to Offense in Auto

insights.claim: US homeowners combined ratio reached 88% in 2025—best since 2006—driven primarily by an absence of major hurricane landfalls, but this favorable result reflects transient factors rather than a structural reset.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Do not extrapolate 2025 homeowners profitability forward; one active hurricane season would materially impair this line and expose under-reserved carriers.
insights.quote: The market approached a turning point in 2025 with an impressive 88% combined ratio. Driving this result, the best since 2006, was an absence of major hurricanes making landfall.
insights.locator: Scale and Underwriting Precision Decisive in Homeowners

insights.claim: Insured losses from severe convective storms (SCS) exceeded $50 billion in each of 2023, 2024, and 2025, versus a prior-decade average of roughly $30 billion per year, and SCS losses have now surpassed traditional hurricane losses as the primary US catastrophe driver.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: SCS is a non-modeled, geographically dispersed peril that is harder to reinsure and price than coastal hurricane; carriers with Midwest/Plains concentrations face structural loss-cost increases.
insights.quote: According to Verisk and Munich Re, insured losses from SCS events exceeded $50 billion in each of 2023, 2024, and 2025, compared with an average of roughly $30 billion a year in the prior decade.
insights.locator: Scale and Underwriting Precision Decisive in Homeowners

insights.claim: Swiss Re Institute projects global insured natural-catastrophe losses will grow at approximately 5% to 7% annually through 2030, fueled by rising asset values in exposed areas and increasing loss severity from climate-related perils.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: A 5–7% annual loss-cost growth rate structurally supports hard property reinsurance pricing over the medium term; long reinsurers, short primary carriers with concentrated exposures.
insights.quote: Swiss Re Institute estimates that global insured natural-catastrophe losses will continue to grow at approximately 5% to 7% annually through 2030
insights.locator: Rethinking the Climate and Catastrophe Exposure Map

insights.claim: Average annual insured losses from billion-dollar US weather events more than doubled between the 2010–2019 decade and the 2020–2024 period, according to NOAA data.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Historical cat models built on pre-2020 data systematically understate expected losses; carriers relying on legacy models face adverse reserve development.
insights.quote: According to NOAA, the average annual insured loss from billion-dollar weather events more than doubled between the 2010–2019 decade and the 2020–2024 period.
insights.locator: Rethinking the Climate and Catastrophe Exposure Map

insights.claim: Reinsurance pricing for property catastrophe moderated with approximately 15–20% premium reductions at 2025 renewals, but terms and conditions remain more restrictive than pre-2022 levels.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Reinsurance cost relief is real but partial; restrictive T&Cs mean primary carriers retain more SCS and secondary-peril exposure than headline rate reductions imply.
insights.quote: approximately 15% to 20% reductions are reported by primary carriers...terms and conditions remain more restrictive than in the years before 2022 levels, according to reinsurance broker Guy Carpenter.
insights.locator: Reinsurance Optimization

themes.theme: Social Inflation and Casualty Reserve Risk

insights.claim: There were 135 nuclear verdicts ($10 million or more) in 2024—the most since Marathon Strategies began tracking in 2009—capping five consecutive years of growth since 2020.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Nuclear verdict frequency is at a structural high, not a cyclical spike; long-tail casualty reserves are almost certainly understated at carriers that have not proactively strengthened.
insights.quote: According to Marathon Strategies...there were 135 nuclear verdicts in 2024, the most since Marathon began tracking the data in 2009, capping five years of consistent growth since 2020.
insights.locator: The Long Shadow of Social Inflation

insights.claim: Third-party litigation financing—in which hedge funds, family offices, and other investors fund plaintiffs' legal costs in exchange for a share of awards—has grown to an estimated $15–18 billion market.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Litigation finance capital is a structural loss-cost accelerant; size of market ($15–18B) suggests the trend is durable, not cyclical—underweight carriers with heavy long-tail commercial liability.
insights.quote: Swiss Re Institute estimates that third-party litigation financing...has grown to an estimated $15 billion to $18 billion market.
insights.locator: The Long Shadow of Social Inflation

insights.claim: US insurers experienced $62 billion in total adverse reserve developments for commercial liability lines (excluding medical professional liability) during the decade 2015–2024, in sharp contrast to 19 consecutive years of favorable aggregate reserve developments for the broader US P&C market.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: $62B in adverse development over one decade signals that casualty pricing and reserving models are systematically broken; expect continued reserve charges at carriers with large commercial auto and GL books.
insights.quote: According to Swiss Re, US insurers experienced $62 billion in total adverse developments during the decade 2015 through 2024 for commercial liability lines
insights.locator: The Long Shadow of Social Inflation

insights.claim: Even as property insurance lines soften, casualty pricing is expected to remain firm, creating a bifurcated rate environment within commercial lines portfolios.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Carriers with casualty-heavy commercial books will benefit from continued rate momentum; pure property specialists face a harder margin environment.
insights.quote: Even as property lines soften, casualty pricing is expected to remain firm, creating a bifurcated rate environment in commercial lines portfolios.
insights.locator: The Long Shadow of Social Inflation

insights.claim: Leading US P&C carriers report 20–30% reductions in commercial lines underwriting cycle times and measurable improvement in loss ratio predictive accuracy from AI models combining structured data with unstructured inputs such as satellite imagery and IoT data.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: AI is already translating into expense ratio and loss ratio advantages; carriers that are still in pilot mode face compounding disadvantage as leaders scale.
insights.quote: Some commercial lines underwriters report reductions in underwriting cycle times of 20% to 30% and measurable improvement in loss ratio predictive accuracy.
insights.locator: Underwriting Augmentation

insights.claim: US P&C carriers deploying AI in claims automation report reductions of 15–25% in claims processing costs in automated segments through AI-driven first-notice-of-loss triage, computer vision damage assessment, and fraud detection.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Claims cost is the largest expense line in P&C; a 15–25% reduction in automated segments creates a durable expense ratio advantage that compounds over time.
insights.quote: Some carriers report reductions of 15% to 25% in claims processing costs in automated segments.
insights.locator: Claims Automation

insights.claim: The gap between AI leaders and laggards in US P&C is already widening; carriers that invested in data infrastructure, model governance, and talent over the past three to five years are now capturing advantages that are difficult to replicate quickly.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: AI moat in insurance is real and widening—early-mover advantage is durable because data pipelines and model governance take years to build; this supports premium valuations for AI-leading carriers.
insights.quote: The gap between AI leaders and laggards in P&C is already widening. Companies that have invested in data infrastructure, model governance, and talent over the past three to five years are now capturing advantages that are difficult to replicate quickly.
insights.locator: AI: From Pilot to Platform

insights.claim: Real-time pricing engines incorporating telematics data, behavioral signals, and external data sources are enabling more precise risk segmentation in personal auto, while commercial lines carriers are building dynamic pricing capabilities that better align pricing with underlying risks.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Telematics-enabled pricing reduces adverse selection and improves loss ratios structurally; long carriers with mature telematics programs, short those relying on traditional credit-based scoring.
insights.quote: Real-time pricing engines that incorporate telematics data, behavioral signals, and external information sources are enabling more specific risk segmentation, particularly in personal auto.
insights.locator: Pricing Optimization

insights.claim: New AI liability coverages have not kept pace with business deployment of AI; carriers are pursuing blanket AI exclusions while the design of innovative policies lags, leaving a significant coverage gap in professional liability, IP, and copyright exposures.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: AI liability is the next cyber—carriers that build loss data and early product lines now will command the emerging market; blanket exclusions are a temporary gap that entrepreneurial carriers or MGAs will fill.
insights.quote: It's early days, but thus far new coverages have not kept pace with businesses' deployment of AI. AI agents represent an emerging frontier, and major carriers recognize the risks.
insights.locator: AI as a New Risk Class

insights.claim: Actuarial data for AI systems takes five to seven years to develop while new AI models come to market in months, creating an insurmountable data gap for traditional actuarial pricing of AI-related risks in the near term.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: This data lag means AI liability pricing will be based on analogical models (cyber, E&O) rather than empirical loss history for years; first movers accept model risk but capture market share.
insights.quote: Actuarial data takes five to seven years to develop, and new AI models come to market in a matter of months.
insights.locator: AI as a New Risk Class

insights.claim: The cyber insurance market's evolution from bounded early policies to full first- and third-party coverage provides a likely roadmap for AI liability insurance development, with early investors in loss data and product development positioned to lead the expanding market.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Watch for the emergence of AI liability specialist carriers or MGAs modeled on early cyber insurers like Coalition or At-Bay; this is a venture-scale opportunity within specialty insurance.
insights.quote: The cyber insurance market's evolution from early temporary bounded policies to full first- and third-party coverage may offer a path forward.
insights.locator: AI as a New Risk Class

insights.claim: Industry surplus in US P&C is at historically high levels and underlying profitability has been restored for most well-run carriers, strengthening the strategic rationale for M&A and driving a shift toward higher-value, more-targeted acquisitions even as deal volume has decreased since 2023.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Excess capital + restored profitability = M&A catalyst; track surplus levels and cash flow generation at mid-size specialty carriers as acquisition indicators.
insights.quote: With underlying profitability restored for most well-run carriers and industry surplus at historically high levels, the strategic rationale for M&A is strengthening.
insights.locator: M&A: A New Wave of Consolidation

insights.claim: The $11 billion merger of Zurich Insurance and Beazley, expected to close later in 2026, illustrates the scale of ambition in P&C M&A, with midsize specialty carriers and excess and surplus platforms identified as the most attractive US targets.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: E&S platforms and specialty MGAs are strategic acquisition targets; position in US E&S-focused carriers and platforms ahead of consolidation.
insights.quote: The $11 billion merger of Zurich Insurance and Beazley, expected to close later this year, illustrates the scale of ambition now on the table.
insights.locator: M&A: A New Wave of Consolidation

insights.claim: Japanese and Korean insurers—Tokio Marine, Sompo, MS&AD, and Samsung Fire & Marine—have demonstrated a sustained appetite for US P&C acquisitions as a source of earnings diversification, and BCG expects this cross-border trend to continue subject to regulatory scrutiny.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Asian insurer demand for US assets provides a structural bid under US P&C targets; this cross-border buyer pool supports acquisition premium multiples.
insights.quote: Japanese and Korean insurers (Tokio Marine, Sompo, MS&AD, and Samsung Fire & Marine) have demonstrated a sustained appetite for acquisitions in the US as a source of earnings diversification and growth.
insights.locator: Cross-Border Activity

insights.claim: Capability acquisition in AI, advanced analytics, and climate modeling is faster and cheaper than organic build, driving premium valuations for target companies with proprietary data assets, managing general agent platforms, and frontier technologies.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: MGA platforms and insurtechs with proprietary data assets command premium M&A multiples; this is a buy-side catalyst for specialty MGA and insurtech positions.
insights.quote: Target companies with proprietary data assets, managing general agent platforms, and frontier technologies command premium valuations.
insights.locator: Capability Acquisition

themes.theme: Scale Economics and Talent as Moats

insights.claim: The top five US personal lines writers continue to extend their market share advantage as fixed costs in claims handling, distribution, and technology increasingly disadvantage smaller carriers.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Scale is a durable competitive moat in personal lines; concentrate in top-5 personal lines writers, avoid sub-scale carriers exposed to the same loss costs without the expense ratio offset.
insights.quote: Fixed costs in claims handling, distribution, and technology are increasingly disadvantaging smaller carriers. The top five personal lines writers continue to extend their market share advantage.
insights.locator: Scale as a Moat

insights.claim: Access to skilled underwriters, data scientists, and AI engineers has emerged as a binding constraint on P&C value creation, with managing general agents, private-equity-backed startups, and technology companies competing in the same talent pools.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/nathalia-bellizia
insights.so_what: Talent scarcity will drive compensation inflation and talent poaching; carriers with strong technical cultures and equity-like incentives will outperform on the capability dimension.
insights.quote: Access to talent has emerged as a binding constraint on value creation. The competition for skilled underwriters, data scientists, and AI engineers has intensified significantly
insights.locator: Secure Scarce Talent
